does: This paper introduces GPTFuzzer, a black-box fuzzing framework that automatically generates adversarial jailbreak prompts to test the safety of large language models. The method adapts techniques from AFL software fuzzing to the domain of LLM red-teaming by starting with human-written jailbreak templates as seeds and iteratively mutating them using semantic-preserving operations. The system employs a seed selection strategy to balance exploration efficiency with template diversity, applies mutation operators that create semantically equivalent or similar sentences, and uses a judgment model to identify successful jailbreaks. By automating jailbreak generation rather than relying on manual crafting, GPTFuzzer enables systematic large-scale testing of LLM vulnerabilities and reveals failure modes that might otherwise remain hidden.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research in adversarial testing of neural networks and safety evaluation of language models. It extends classical fuzzing techniques from software security, particularly American Fuzzy Lop, to the novel domain of LLM robustness. The paper responds to the limitation of manually-crafted jailbreak templates documented in prior work on prompt injection attacks and adversarial prompting. It connects to earlier research on finding distribution-shift vulnerabilities in deep learning systems and automated testing methodologies. The work also engages with concurrent studies examining how LLMs can be manipulated through clever prompt engineering, and builds on recognition that safety-training measures alone prove insufficient against determined adversarial approaches.
enables: This research enables more efficient and comprehensive red-teaming of LLMs by removing the manual bottleneck in jailbreak discovery, making it practical to systematically probe large model populations for safety vulnerabilities. It points toward automated evaluation frameworks that continuously test deployed systems, similar to ongoing fuzzing in software security. The work could facilitate development of more robust safety training methods by providing systematic datasets of successful jailbreaks. Future directions include applying the framework to emerging model architectures, combining automated fuzzing with interpretability techniques to understand why particular prompts succeed, and developing defensive measures informed by systematic adversarial probe results. The fuzzing approach could extend to other domains where neural systems require safety validation, establishing a foundation for automated adversarial evaluation as a standard practice in AI deployment.
